title: Braided rush log basket
subtitle: A coiled and sewn log carrier from twisted and braided dried rush
excerpt: A log basket made by coiling and sewing a braid of twisted rush is a traditional technique that requires no frame or stakes. The braid is made continuously and coiled in a flat base, then the sides are built up by stacking coils. The result is a robust, flexible carrier suited to holding and carrying firewood. A beginner project in coiled basketry.
sourceNotes: Coiled rush and straw basketry documented in British domestic craft tradition; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) and in Rural Industries Bureau basketry records.

glossaryTerms.definition: Common bulrush or sea grass, dried and twisted for coiled basketry. In a log basket, the rush is formed into a three-strand braid that becomes the coil element sewn into the basket form.
A basketry construction method in which a continuous bundle or braid is coiled in a spiral and each round is sewn to the round below, building up the base and sides without any frame or stakes.
The thin strips of rush or raffia used to bind one coil to the coil below. Each stitch passes through a gap in the lower coil and loops around the new coil to lock the two together.
A blunt-tipped pointed tool used to open gaps in the coil for the sewing rush to pass through when binding each new round of coiling.

projectSchedule.title: Braid and coil the base
projectSchedule.body: Make the braid and coil the base to 35 cm diameter. Two to three hours.

projectSchedule.title: Build the sides and add handles
projectSchedule.body: Stack and sew the side coils to 30 cm height, sew on two twisted-rush handles. Two to three more hours.

projectSchedule.title: Basket dried and firm
projectSchedule.body: After two days of drying the coils contract and the basket is rigid.

content.text: basket builds from a continuous braided element sewn in a spiral. There are no stakes to prepare and no frame to set up: the basket grows from the centre of the base outward and then upward as each coil is added and
to the one below. A log basket at 35 cm in diameter and 30 cm in height is large enough to hold a full armload of wood and strong enough to carry it.

Dried bulrush or sea grass in two thicknesses: thick bundles for the braided coil core, and thin flat strips for the sewing element. A generous quantity is needed for a log basket: expect to use 500 to 700 g of dried rush total. All material should be dampened before use: wrap in a damp towel for fifteen to twenty minutes.
for opening the coil to pass the sewing element through. A large-eyed blunt needle or a bodkin needle threaded with thin rush strips. No other tools required.

Gather three equal bundles of rush, each about 1.5 cm in diameter when compressed. Tie them together at one end with a thin strip of rush. Braid the three bundles in a standard three-strand plait, keeping the braid even and firm. Add new rush to any bundle as it thins: insert a new handful of damp rush into the bundle and continue braiding, overlapping the old and new material for 5 to 6 cm before the old is exhausted. The braid is made continuously throughout the basket; stop making braid only when adding coils is stopped.

Coil the knotted starting end of the braid into a tight flat circle about 5 to 6 cm in diameter. Thread a sewing rush strip through the bodkin needle and sew the coil to itself with a figure-eight stitch, passing the needle through the inner coil, around the outer coil, and back. The stitches should be about 2 cm apart. This starting centre must be tight; a loose centre will result in a misshapen base.

Continue adding braid to the outer edge of the coil, sewing each new round to the round inside it with the figure-eight stitch and the bodkin needle. Work flat (on the lap) until the base is 35 cm in diameter. To check flatness, press the base down on a flat surface: any rocking indicates a section has been sewn too tightly and is pulling up. Correct by easing the stitches in that area.

To transition from base to sides, begin angling the new coil up at about 45 degrees from the previous outer edge coil rather than laying it flat. The angle gradually increases to 90 degrees (vertical) over the first two rounds of side coiling. From that point, hold the new coil directly on top of the outer edge of the previous coil rather than outside it, and sew as before. The basket sides rise with each new coil until they reach 30 cm height.
Step 5: Finish the top and add handles

Taper the braid at the end of the top coil by gradually removing rush from each of the three braid strands over the last 15 cm so that the braid narrows to a point. Sew the taper tightly into the top coil. For handles, make two short braids of 30 to 35 cm and sew their ends firmly to opposite sides of the basket at the top coil, with the handle arching 10 to 12 cm above the rim. Use several stitches on each end to distribute the load.
Leave to dry for two days on a flat surface. The rush will contract as it dries and the basket will firm and tighten. No additional finish is needed or appropriate for a log carrier.
Make a smaller version (20 cm diameter, 15 cm high) for a bread basket or fruit bowl using the same technique at reduced scale.
Use dyed rush strips for the sewing element in a contrasting colour to make the stitch pattern a decorative feature of the finished basket.

items.symptom: The base domes upward rather than lying flat
items.cause: The inner coils were sewn too tightly, pulling the centre up, or the braid was twisted rather than lying flat during coiling.
items.fix: Press the base firmly under a heavy weight (a stack of books) while still damp. If the dome persists after drying, dampen the base and repeat.

items.symptom: The stitches are visible and uneven on the outside
items.cause: The sewing element is too thick, or stitches were placed irregularly.
items.fix: Use a finer strip of rush for sewing. Aim for stitches every 2 cm and check spacing against a ruler for the first few rounds until the rhythm is established.

items.symptom: The braid separates into its component strands during side building
items.cause: The sewing stitches do not grip the braid firmly enough to hold it under the weight of the log basket sides.
items.fix: Increase the number of stitches per round on the sides (every 1.5 cm rather than 2 cm) and use a sewing element that wraps all the way around the coil rather than passing through it.
